title: For viruses, eavesdropping doesn’t always go according to plan
html_body:
Today in Science and science: The magic of gravitational lensing, bumble bees have rhythm, and more
Brought to you by the Gairdner Foundation
View this email in your browser
3 April 2026
Today’s Visualized takes a look at the wacky animals that lived at the end of the Ediacaran Period. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including some manipulative viruses and how lensed supernovae could resolve a dispute between astronomers and cosmologists.
space  |  News feature
Gravitational lensing could break the Hubble tension
How do you measure the distance to far off cosmic objects? That question is key to calculating the speed of the universe’s expansion and hence understanding its evolution and eventual fate. The rate of cosmic expansion, known as the Hubble constant, is so important for cosmologists that the disagreement among researchers over its value has its own name: the Hubble tension. Astronomers measure it one way, using stars or supernovae with predictable brightness. Cosmologists have another way, studying ripples in the echo of the Big Bang and winding the clock forward to today. The two techniques have become increasingly precise, but they steadfastly disagree with each other.

A third method is needed to break the deadlock . That may come through the magic of gravitational lensing, which can cause a supernova—a star exploding at the end of its life—to appear to explode again and again. If a supernova is situated behind a large mass, such as a galaxy or cluster of galaxies, then as its light passes by the mass, its gravity bends the light along different paths, producing multiple images that show the explosion at different times when viewed from Earth. Using the time delays and path lengths, researchers can calculate the distance to the supernova and so calculate the Hubble constant.

Only a handful of such lensed supernovae have been found so far, but several upcoming survey telescopes, including the Vera C. Rubin Observatory, are expected to find them by the dozen and, hopefully, to ease the Hubble tension.
listen to the Science Podcast
Read the full story
comparative cognition  |  news from Science
Bumble bees show a surprising knack for rhythm
You know this little dude would crush it at Just Dance.  bee lab at southern medical university
Bumble bees are hardly nature’s most graceful creatures, and their name reflects it. But it turns out these critters show a surprising knack for rhythm. The fuzzy insects can recognize a rhythm and also identify the same pattern when scientists change the tempo, according to research published yesterday in Science —the first time this ability has been documented outside of a few mammals and birds.

Researchers at Southern Medical University in Guangzhou, China, decided to test the remarkable cognitive abilities of bees by evaluating their ability to follow a beat. The scientists placed bumblebees in a box with two clear cups turned upside down, each of which contained a green LED. The lights blinked with brief, repeating patterns: for example, long, long, short, short—or long, short, long, short. One sequence signaled a reward, a puddle of sugar water on top of the cup, while the other did not. After learning the two rhythms, the bees tended to land on the cup associated with the reward, guided by the enticing rhythm. And when the researchers sped up or slowed down the tempo of the flashes, the bees still recognized the rhythmic pattern associated with a sugar hit and landed on that cup.

Insects including crickets and fireflies can mimic other individuals’ chirps and flashes. But the ability to recognize the same rhythms had long been assumed to be a complex mental task requiring a large brain. “Imagine you’re listening to a song, and it’s slowed down or sped up, but you can still recognize it,” said study co-author Cwyn Solvi. “That’s not because you’ve memorized one single detail, but because you’ve grasped the whole structure.”
read the Science Paper
Read the Full Story
microbiology  |  cell
For viruses, eavesdropping doesn’t always go according to plan
Bacteriophages are viruses that infect bacteria, and once they do, they don’t always immediately start replicating. Although bursting open an initial victim is advantageous when many nearby cells remain to be infected, it can be risky when hosts are scarce and new infections are unlikely to succeed. In those cases, the phage can instead remain dormant.

To help make that decision, some phages release small peptide signals that accumulate as infections spread. High concentrations signal that nearby hosts have already been depleted, while low concentrations suggest plenty of fresh cells to infect.

But the phage that emitted the signal isn’t the only one that can detect it. In a study published in Cell, researchers found that unrelated phages can intercept each other’s signals and respond. Rather than ignoring foreign signals, viruses often change their behavior, becoming more likely to remain dormant when they detect peptides released by another species.

To test this, the researchers exposed bacteria to multiple phage species that use this type of communication, tracking how each virus responded to signals produced by the other. By varying signal peptide concentrations and combinations, they could measure how cross-species detection factored into the decision-making process.

It turns out it’s not always smooth coordination. Because the signal reflects a different virus’s environment, the responding virus may switch strategies at the wrong time, missing opportunities to replicate when hosts are still abundant. Meanwhile, the signaling virus may benefit, effectively suppressing competitors by nudging them into inactivity. “ In other words, viral communication is not just cooperation,” lead study author Robyn Manley said in a statement. “Sometimes, it is manipulation.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
2026 Canada Gairdner Awards Honour Breakthrough Science
Meet the 2026 laureates recognized for groundbreaking discoveries advancing human health, from fundamental biology to global health research, with advances that are transforming our understanding of disease and shaping the future of medicine.
Read Now
visualized
An artist’s reconstruction of organisms discovered at the Jiangchuan fossil site, as they might have appeared about 554 to 539 million years ago. Note the tall, cylindrical “bugle worms” anchored to the seafloor by disc-shaped bottoms.  xiaodong wang
Scientists have long viewed the Cambrian explosion, which began roughly 535 million years ago, as a kind of biological Big Bang. Life underwent a rapid and dramatic diversification during this period, so traditional thinking goes, and produced the ancestors of most major types of animals. A massive treasure trove of fossils discovered in southwest China, however, suggests this evolutionary explosion actually began much earlier, at the end of the Ediacaran Period.

In a new study in Science, researchers describe hundreds of fossil specimens recovered from Jiangchuan Biota in Yunnan Province. The site revealed a spectacular array of soft-bodied critters from between 554 and 539 million years ago, capturing the transition between the Ediacaran and Cambrian and demonstrating that many key animal groups had already evolved before the start of the latter period. The team was especially intrigued to identify animals with bilateral symmetry, including fossils thought to be the oldest known relatives of deuterostomes—an expansive group that today includes humans and other vertebrates.
The artist’s reconstruction (right) of a deuterostome cambroernid fossil (left) depicts the animal’s sausage-shaped body, attached to the seafloor by a stalk, and tentacles for catching food.  gaorong li and xiaodong Wang
“Our discovery closes a major gap in the earliest phases of animal diversification,” lead study author Gaorong Li says in a statement. “For the first time, we demonstrate that many complex animals, normally only found in the Cambrian, were present in the Ediacaran period, meaning that they evolved much earlier than previously demonstrated by fossil evidence.”

While most Ediacaran fossil sites preserve organisms as mere impressions, the Jiangchuan Biota specimens take the form of compressed carbon films, which showcase feeding structures, guts, and locomotory organs in exquisite detail. Researchers identified tall, cylindrical “bugle worms” and a creature that vaguely resembles a tentacled martini glass. Some of these fossils are truly wacky, with combinations of anatomical features that don’t match any known Ediacaran or Cambrian species. According to study co-author Frankie Dunn, one even bears a striking resemblance to the sand worm from Dune.

Altogether, the findings provide new information about the rise of complex animal life and demonstrate what study co-author Luke Parry describes as “the weird world of the Ediacaran giving way to the Cambrian.”
Read the Paper
podcast
Resolving the dispute over the speed of the expanding universe, and seeking new drug targets for cognitive disfunction
By SARAH CRESPI, elah feder, kevin mclean, daniel clery   |   2 April 2026
Et Cetera
Hey, big spender
According to one key measure, China has surpassed the United States in terms of research and development spending. “This is important, but not especially surprising,” said one policy specialist. “It feels less like a sudden overtaking moment and more like the continuation of a long-running trend.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
Sunken treasure
Marine archaeologists have discovered a Danish warship that sank more than 200 years ago during the Battle of Copenhagen. Now, the team is racing to unearth the wreck from the seabed before the area becomes a construction site.
Read more at Associated Press
The riddle of the sphinx
Scientists have used a neural network based on a nematode worm to control a digital fruit fly’s body. This “digital sphinx” is meant to serve as a cautionary tale about the interpretation of connectome models.
BiorXiv Preprint  |  Read more at The Transmitter
Last but not least
In honor of International Children’s Book Day, I’d like to repromote this iconic What We’re Enjoying from 2024, which featured reviews from Science staff and their kids. If you ask me, Everyone Poops will never cease to be a classic. 
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Daniel Clery, Michael Greshko, and Ana Georgescu
